                 Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                 Gloria P. Green, Associate Editor
                 James A. McCall, Associate Editor


                                                                Changes in table formats

                             Beginning with the August 1973 issue, all tables show data in historical sequence, left to right or top
                          to bottom. For most tables, this means that the earliest data appear in the column closest to the stub,
                          the latest data in the column farthest from the stub. In tables where the data periods are in the stub,
                          the earliest data appear on the top line, the latest data on the bottom line.

           to increase in the third quarter of 1973, although at a
            slower pace than over the past 2 years. The third-quarter                 The moderate labor force growth recorded in the

           cent. The unemployment rate has shown some improve-                    groups, the participation rate for adult women rose
           ment in every quarter since late 1971. (See table A-41.)               slightly (from 44.4 to 44.7 percent), and that for
               Among the persons outside the labor force, there was               teenagers declined (from 54.2 to 52.7 percent). The
           a noticeable decline in the number of "discouraged                     increase in participation for women was in line with
           workers"—those who want work but are not seeking                       their historical trend, while the decline for teenagers
           jobs because they think their search would be in vain.                 was an interruption of the strong upward movement

              NOTE: N.E.C. is an abbreviation for "not elsewhere classified" and designates broad categories of occupations which cannot be more
           specifically identified. For example, carpenters who are foremen are classified as "carpenters," but general foremen without specific
           occupational responsibilities are classified as "foremen, n.e.c."

             tAs noted on page 7 of the January 1972 issue of this magazine, the series does not include earned pay withheld as a consequence of delays caused by the wage freeze in effect between August 15
          and November 14, 1971, and by administrative procedures of the Pay Board. These delays resulted from compliance with the Government's Economic Stabilization Program. Because these withheld
          payments were substantial and fell due under a significant number of new labor-management contracts in that industry ,they affected a large proportion of SIC 372. The series including these pay-
          ments for Sept. 1971-June 1972 are shown below.
             A 17 -cent delayed payment for employees in a number of large companies is still pending as a consequence of litigation in the courts arising out of the Government's Economic Stabilization Pro-
          gram. When the issue is settled, these data will be revised, if necessary, to include this additional delayed payment.

              The statistics in this periodical are compiled from three major     whereas detailed industrial classifications can be reliably derived
           sources: (1) Household interviews, (2) reports from employers,         only from establishment reports.
           and (3) administrative statistics of unemployment insurance                Data from these two sources differ from each other because
           systems.                                                               of differences in definition and coverage, sources of information,

           reporting a week of unemployment under an unemployment                       months prior to the week of reference. Initial claims are notices
           Insurance program. It includes some persons who are working                  filed by those losing jobs covered by an unemployment
           part time who would be counted as employed in the payroll and                insurance program that they are starting a period of unemploy-
           household surveys. Excluded are persons who have exhausted                   ment. A claimant who continued to be unemployed a full week
           their benefit rights and workers who have not earned rights to               is then counted in the insured unemployment figure.
           unemployment insurance. In general, excluded from coverage are                    Because of differences in State laws and procedures under

           family work, selected nonprofit organizations, some State and                unemployment insurance programs are operated, State
           local government and self-employment. Also excluded from the                 unemployment rates generally indicate, but do not precisely
           insured unemployment count, but included as employed in the                  measure, differences among the individual States. Persons
           household survey, are those persons who earned no wages during               wishing to receive a detailed description of the nature, sources,

               Seasonally adjusted job vacancy rates are computed by             The revised seasonally adjusted series for the establishment
           dividing the seasonally adjusted number of job vacancies by the    data reflect experience through March 1973. Seasonal factors to
           sum of seasonally adjusted employment and job vacancies and        be used for current adjustment appear in the June 1973
           multiplying the quotient by 100. Seasonally adjusted long-term     Employment and Earnings.
           job vacancy rates are computed by dividing the seasonally
           adjusted long-term job vacancies by the sum of seasonally
           adjusted' employment and total job vacancies and multiplying
           the quotient by 100.
